dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 8379 IH: FIRE Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Ms. Letlow introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform

form.official-title: To prohibit diversity, equity, and inclusion in Federal hiring and employment, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Freedom from Ideological Requirements in Employment Act or the FIRE Act.

section.header: Prohibition on diversity, equity, and inclusion in Federal hiring and employment

subsection.text: No Federal funds may be obligated or expended to—

paragraph.text: require, as a condition for appointment to, or continued employment in, the civil service (as that term is defined in section 2101 of title 5, United States Code)—

subparagraph.text: diversity, equity, and inclusion training; or
any individual to sign, endorse, or otherwise accent statements that are centered on diversity, equity, and inclusion principles; or

paragraph.text: develop, implement, distribute, plan, or purchase training courses for the Federal workforce that relates to—

subparagraph.text: critical theory relating to race and gender;
intersectionality, sexual orientation, or gender identity; or

the assertion that a particular race, color, ethnicity, religion, biological sex, or national origin is inherently or systemically superior, inferior, oppressive, oppressed, privileged, or unprivileged.

subsection.text: Nothing in this section shall be construed to apply to commonly accepted and customarily used hiring or employment practices that prevent sexual harassment in the workplace.

subsection.header: Definition of diversity, equity, and inclusion
subsection.text: In this section, the term diversity, equity, and inclusion means any practice, training, statement, or principle that asserts—

paragraph.text: a particular race, color, ethnicity, religion, biological sex, or national origin is inherently or systemically superior or inferior, oppressive or oppressed, or privileged or unprivileged; or
how systemic racism is embedded in legal systems, policies, and societal structures rather than being solely a product of individual prejudice.
